exemplar.title: To what was Boeing successful restoring their brand image following the Boeing 737 MAX catastrophes?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what was Boeing successful restoring their brand image following the Boeing 737 MAX catastrophes?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: The topic is communicated adequately with sufficient explanation and supported by visual exhibits (timeline, financial tables).
The research question is clearly stated and consistently linked to the analysis throughout the essay.
A generally appropriate range of relevant sources is employed, demonstrating informed selection of primary and secondary materials.
weaknesses: Occasional grammatical slips and some repetition detract from precision and conciseness.
The introduction and aim statement restate background narrative rather than sharpening focus on concrete objectives.
The methodology lacks systematic justification for how quantitative versus qualitative sources were measured in relation to brand image restoration.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Focus and Method is placed in the midâlevel of the 3â4 band (score of 3). The student communicates the topic and research question clearly and chooses relevant sources, but lapses in precision, occasional drift into narrative, and an under-specified methodological justification prevent the work from reaching the upper band.

strengths: Key Business Management terminology is generally used correctly and shows familiarity with the subject lexicon.
Source material is mostly relevant and appropriately applied to illustrate points about reputational impact.
The essay demonstrates clear and coherent subject knowledge, integrating theoretical models with case examples.
weaknesses: Inconsistent citation formatting, occasional misspellings, and lapses in terminology precision reduce clarity.
Some source applications are descriptive rather than analytically linked to the argument.
Limited triangulation of conflicting perspectives and occasional factual oversimplifications weaken depth of understanding.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and Understanding sits securely at the midpoint of the 3â4 band (score of 3). The work applies terminology and sources competently and conveys coherent knowledge, but inconsistencies in usage and depth of source integration keep it from the higher band.

strengths: A logical, wellâdeveloped central argument is sustained from theory application through to evaluation.
Research draws on a reasonable breadth of primary documents, regulatory filings, financial data and scholarly commentary all clearly linked to the research question.
Analysis moves beyond description with clear causeâandâeffect reasoning and supported conclusions.
weaknesses: Critical appraisal of source reliability and bias is limited, particularly regarding corporate press releases and polls.
Depth of analysis is uneven: financial metrics are presented without full interpretation of their impact on brand perception.
Counterâarguments and external confounding factors (e.g., COVID-19) receive insufficient examination, and the evaluation section largely restates prior analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Critical Thinking is placed in the midâlevel of the 4â6 band (score of 4). The student constructs a coherent, relevant argument with clear analysis, but insufficient critical scrutiny of sources, uneven depth, and limited engagement with alternative perspectives prevent placement in the higher band.

strengths: All required sections and subsections are present and logically ordered, aiding navigation.
Headings, subheadings, page numbering and exhibits are correctly applied, providing a functional framework.
Basic academic formatting conventions (tables, italics, bibliography) are in place and generally clear.
weaknesses: Inconsistencies in citation formatting and some typographical errors (e.g., âdeltâ, mixed list styles) detract from professionalism.
Several exhibits lack fully labeled axes or consistent caption styling.
Occasional lapses in proofreadingâsuch as duplicated entries and mid-sentence fragmentâundermine polish and readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation merits a score of 3 in the 3â4 band. The structure and layout meet core requirements and guide the reader effectively, but inconsistencies in styling, labeling and proofreading keep it from the top of the band.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Clarity and focus** â The research question is explicitly stated several times and remains the thread linking the introduction, analysis and conclusion. Despite an awkward grammatical construction, its intent (âTo what extent was Boeing successfulâ¦â) is unmistakable.

**Connection to discussion** â All major sections (SCCT analysis, IRT application, stakeholder/shareholder reactions, financial data) are organised around assessing Boeingâs image restoration, demonstrating a sustained if occasionally descriptive linkage between the RQ and the discussion.

**Why not 5-6?** â Although focused, the wording lacks precision (missing âextentâ), and the essay sometimes drifts into narrative of events rather than maintaining a tight analytical focus on âsuccessâ criteria, so the effectiveness is adequate rather than consistently strong. Hence the top of the 3-4 band is appropriate.

**Argument development** â The essay follows a logical progression from context to theory application, stakeholder reactions, financial assessment, and a final judgement on image restoration. Each section contributes to answering the RQ, yielding a **well-developed central argument** that Boeingâs efforts were largely ineffective.

**Evaluation** â The student integrates multiple perspectives (company statements, regulators, stakeholders, financial data) and offers some critical reflection on the timing and sincerity of Boeingâs responses. The concluding evaluation revisits evidence and draws reasoned, if cautious, conclusions.

**Limitations** â Evaluation could probe deeper into counter-arguments (e.g., partial recovery in 2023) and weigh qualitative brand-perception data more rigorously. Nonetheless, the discussion moves beyond description to partial critical evaluation, warranting a solid 5 in the 4-6 band.

**Demonstrated understanding** â The student shows clear knowledge of crisis-management theories (SCCT, IRT) and business concepts such as stakeholder reactions, financial performance, and contingency planning. Definitions are accurate and examples from Boeing are used to illustrate concepts.

**Coherence** â The narrative generally integrates historical context, theoretical models and financial data to construct an argument about image restoration. The discussion of the three SCCT crisis clusters and the mapping of Boeingâs responses to âdenialâ and ârebuildingâ show a solid grasp of subject matter.

**Weaknesses** â Some theoretical explanations are brief or repetitive, and occasional factual oversimplifications limit depth. Nevertheless, the overall knowledge is coherent and largely well supported, warranting a high mark within the 3-4 band.

**Accuracy of terminology** â Key Business Management terms such as stakeholders, shareholders, crisis management, contingency planning, reputational risk, and corrective action are generally used correctly, indicating familiarity with the subjectâs lexicon.

**Consistency and precision** â While most terms are accurate, there are lapses (e.g., âTo what was Boeing successfulâ¦â, confusion between âloss of revenueâ and âoperating cash flowâ, inconsistent use of âshareholder/stakeholderâ). Some theoretical labels are misspelled (e.g., âadâ instead of âandâ; âdeltâ) and citations are inconsistently formatted.

**Overall** â Terminology use is more accurate than not, but inconsistency and occasional errors reduce clarity, placing the work at the midpoint of the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

**Analytical approach** â The student links SCCT crisis clusters and IRT strategies to Boeingâs actions and tracks financial and stakeholder outcomes, demonstrating a clear cause-and-effect analysis rather than mere description.

**Support for claims** â Assertions about reputational damage are supported with revenue tables, market-loss charts and survey data. The analysis moves beyond narrative by interpreting how denial followed by corrective action undermined credibility.

**Shortcomings** â Depth is uneven: financial figures are presented but not fully dissected; alternative explanations (e.g., COVID-19âs impact) receive little attention; metrics of âbrand imageâ remain implicit. The reasoning is therefore clear and generally supported, but not sophisticated, meriting a lower score within the 4-6 band.

**Relevance and integration** â The essay cites numerous credible sources and incorporates them through quotations, financial tables and theoretical references (e.g., SCCT, IRT). These materials generally illuminate the discussion of Boeingâs image-restoration efforts.

**Appropriateness of use** â Source information is used mostly to illustrate points (e.g., revenue figures, stakeholder polls, DOJ statements) and is usually linked to arguments about reputational impact. This demonstrates a fair level of relevance and appropriate application.

**Limitations** â Several citations are duplicated or incomplete, and some evidence is presented descriptively without clear analytical linkage. There is limited triangulation or critical appraisal of conflicting perspectives. This places the work securely in the mid-level (3) of the 3-4 band.

**Range and relevance of sources/methods** â The student employs a broad mix of primary documents (Boeing statements, FAA releases, U.S. Department of Justice filings) and secondary materials (textbook, academic papers, reputable news outlets). This range is clearly appropriate to a Business Management EE on corporate crisis handling.

**Evidence of informed selection** â The methodology section explains why each category of source was chosen (primary for factual accuracy, secondary for theoretical framing) and recognises limitations in the core textbook, prompting further research. Exhibits with financial and market data show purposeful selection of quantitative evidence.

**Minor weaknesses** â Source evaluation remains largely descriptive, and the method for measuring âbrand image restorationâ is not systematically justified. These omissions prevent the work from reaching the very top of the band, but the breadth and rationale merit a solid score of 5 in the 5-6 band.

**Breadth and relevance** â The investigation gathers official statements, regulatory documents, financial data and scholarly commentary, all directly pertinent to evaluating Boeingâs image recovery. This demonstrates a reasonable breadth of research that is clearly linked to the RQ.

**Evidence of selection** â Primary sources (DOJ filings, FAA directives) and quantitative exhibits are chosen to substantiate claims about accountability and financial impact. This shows clear relevance, though the student sometimes accepts sources at face value without critical scrutiny.

**Why mid-band** â The corpus is good but not exhaustive, and critical appraisal of reliability or bias (e.g., corporate press releases) is limited. Hence the strand meets the descriptors for 4-6 but at a satisfactory rather than exemplary level, justifying a score of 4.

**Formatting adherence** â The student supplies a title page, table of contents, page numbering, word count and bibliography, aligning with core EE presentation requirements. Visuals are labelled as exhibits and integrated into the text.

**Quality of layout** â Basic academic conventions (headings, italics, tables) are applied, but there are inconsistencies: duplicated bibliography entries, occasional typographical errors, and uneven citation formatting. Visuals are sometimes blurry or lack precise sourcing.

Overall, layout and formatting are acceptable and functional but fall short of the âconsistently correctâ standard implied by the top of the band; thus a score of 3 within the 3-4 band is appropriate.

**Strengths** â The student states the topic clearly in the title, aim and introduction, and provides pertinent background on Boeing, the 737 MAX crashes and the relevance of crisis-management theories. The reader can easily grasp what the essay will investigate and why it matters for Business Management.

**Explanation** â Key terms such as âbrand imageâ, âcrisis managementâ, SCCT and IRT are introduced and briefly defined, giving sufficient context for a non-specialist to follow the discussion. Visual exhibits (timeline, financial tables) further clarify the context and significance of the issue.

**Limitations** â Occasional grammatical slips (e.g. âTo what was Boeing successfulâ¦â) and some repetition detract from absolute precision, and the opening rationale could have been more concise. Nonetheless, the communication and explanation are more than adequate, justifying the upper mark in the 3-4 band.

**Organisation** â The essay includes all required sections (title page with word count, table of contents, introduction, methodology, analysis, conclusion, bibliography). Headings and sub-headings guide the reader, and exhibits are numbered and referenced.

**Sequencing and coherence** â The structure broadly follows a logical flow; however, some sections overlap (e.g., âCrisis Managementâ vs. âCompanyâs reactionâ), and there are occasional digressions and repetition that weaken overall coherence.

Given that the framework is appropriate but not consistently polished, the work meets the criteria for a clear though imperfect structure, justifying the lower mark in the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: Footnotes are inconsistently styled and placed; standardize footnote formatting or consider endnotes for a cleaner presentation.
Exhibit 2 presents relevant financial data, but the report needs to explain explicitly why metrics such as operating cash flow directly inform the assessment of brand image restoration.
The quoted Justice Department statement is well chosen, but it requires critical commentary on its reliability and potential corporate bias.
The transition to IRT strategies needs explicit mapping from the previously identified SCCT cluster; ensure each strategy is linked back to Boeingâs crisis profile.
The verb âdeltâ is a typographical error; it should read âdealtâ. Careful proofreading will enhance clarity and professionalism.
The historical background is detailed but partially irrelevant; tighten the introduction by focusing more directly on crisis management theory context.
The methodology identifies source types but lacks justification for selecting quantitative versus qualitative data and defining a method to measure âbrand image restoration.â
The transition into the Crisis Management section is abrupt; add a clear topic sentence linking this discussion back to the research question.
The CNN poll is cited to illustrate stakeholder trust, but the methodology and sample limitations should be evaluated to strengthen the argument.
The use of distinct subheadings for âStakeholder responseâ and âShareholder responseâ effectively guides the reader through different perspectives.
The bar chartâs y-axis mixes positive and negative values without clear labeling; add axis titles and clarify that negative bars indicate market losses.
âincompetenceâsâ is the incorrect form; it should read âincompetencies.â Correcting such errors will improve readability.
The list of SCCT clusters uses a nonstandard list style; use a properly numbered list to improve readability and reinforce the structure.
Asserting Boeing fits the âaccidentâ category lacks engagement with counter-arguments such as regulatory oversights; include a critical evaluation of alternative perspectives.
Bibliography entries display inconsistent styling of italics, author order, and date placement; apply a single citation format (e.g., APA) consistently.
Page numbering appears to be missing from the footer; ensure consistent page numbers on all pages for ease of reference.
Section titles in the contents use italics inconsistently; apply uniform formatting to improve professionalism and consistency.
In-text citations are inconsistently formatted and lack page or paragraph numbers; adopt a consistent citation style throughout the report.
When defining âRebuildingâ from Benoit (2004), include a full citation (page or section) to demonstrate precise engagement with the source.
The narrative on leadership change and settlement outcomes is informative but lacks critical interrogation of stakeholder reactions or long-term impacts.
The âShareholder responseâ paragraph ends mid-sentence, indicating missing content; ensure every section is fully developed before submission.
The Evaluation largely restates prior analysis without introducing new insights or implications; deepen it by reflecting on theoretical lessons for future crisis response.
Exhibit 4âs financial overview needs explicit linkage to theories of brand image restoration; explain how each metric informs stakeholdersâ perceptions.

The research question wording is grammatically awkward. Revise to "To what extent was Boeing successful in restoring its brand image following the Boeing 737 MAX catastrophes?" to improve clarity and precision.
The description of corrective action cites Muilenbergâs apology but lacks assessment of stakeholder reception; include survey or press reaction to evaluate effectiveness.
The first heading is a generic subject label rather than the investigationâs title; consider making âBoeing and the 737 MAX catastrophes â¦â the prominent title to clearly communicate focus.
The word count is provided and falls within the 4 000-word limit, demonstrating adherence to formatting requirements.
The table of contents clearly lists all major sections with corresponding page numbers, aiding navigation through the investigation.
The bolstering section outlines the tactic but does not compare Boeingâs approach to industry best practices; consider adding a brief benchmark for context.
Claims about production and software improvements rely solely on Boeing sources; include independent industry reports or third-party data to substantiate recovery claims.
Several sentences are lengthy with multiple clauses; break into shorter sentences and check punctuation to improve readability.
Ensure consistent styling of major headings (h2) throughout, including font size and spacing, to maintain a polished presentation.
The aim statement merely restates the research question; consider specifying concrete objectives or methods to sharpen focus and guide the analysis.
Exhibit 3âs tabular data duplicates chart information; consider summarizing key figures in the text rather than repeating large tables.
The analysis does not critically assess external factors (e.g., COVID-19) that could have influenced market losses; integrate deeper analysis of confounding variables.
Content on denial and corrective action overlaps earlier sections; consolidate repeated points to improve conciseness and flow.
Caption for Exhibit 4 is not styled consistently with earlier exhibits; bold the label and align formatting to maintain uniformity.
The bibliography contains duplicate entries (e.g., Hoang, 2022 appears twice); consolidate repeated sources for a cleaner reference list.
Some academic sources lack DOI or publisher details; include complete publication information to enhance credibility and traceability.
